intro: Technical IT calculations are fundamental: latency, bandwidth, uptime SLA, cloud costs, algorithmic complexity. A good initial calculation prevents over-provisioning infrastructure. Calculate the time it takes for DNS changes to propagate across the internet with our free, fast tool. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you up to date.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **Propagation Time ≈ Maximum Upstream TTL**. Direct application with your input values. Calculate DNS propagation time instantly.
useCases: Technical calculations related to DNS TTL and propagation

**Technical IT calculators** for latency, storage, uptime SLA, cloud costs—decisions that scale.

## How It's Calculated

Calculate the time it takes for DNS changes to propagate across the internet. Fast, free, and accurate.

This calculator applies the standard formula with your input values, checking typical ranges and alerting you to unreasonable values.

## Final Notes

This calculation is for reference only. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: Should I use a low TTL?
faq.a: Use 300 seconds (5 minutes) if you plan frequent DNS changes. Lower TTLs allow faster updates but increase DNS queries.

faq.q: Should I use a high TTL?
faq.a: Use 86400 seconds (24 hours) to reduce DNS queries and server load. Best for stable, rarely-changed records.
Yes. ISP caches may bypass your TTL setting. If changes are urgent, you may need to wait longer or contact your ISP.
CDN content updates typically go through API, not DNS. CDN and DNS TTL are usually independent.
Services like Cloudflare and Route 53 use Anycast to minimize latency globally. TTL still applies to DNS records.
